prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I have everything I need. The feedback log even contains a directly analogous incident (ENG-204) involving the exact same type of seeding route. Here is the complete diagnosis.

---

## Diagnosis

**The file you edited is not the content.** It is a seeding helper вҖ” a Next.js API route whose job is to call Payload's local API and *write* content into the database. Editing the TypeScript source of that route changes what the seeder *would* push the next time it is called. It does not change what is currently stored in the database, and it does not change what the live site renders. The live site reads exclusively from Supabase Postgres via Payload вҖ” it never reads from `.ts` source files at runtime.

---

## Root Cause вҖ” AP: File-Edit вү  Data-Write (unnamed, closely mirrors ENG-204)

The architectural trap here is a **three-layer confusion** that is easy to fall into:

```
Layer 1 вҖ” Source file    src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts
                         вҶ“  compiled by Next.js build
Layer 2 вҖ” API endpoint   GET/POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro
                         вҶ“  only when the endpoint is CALLED
Layer 3 вҖ” Database row   Supabase Postgres вҶ’ projects collection вҶ’ ETRO record
                         вҶ“  read by every page render
Layer 4 вҖ” Browser        Live site shows the content in Layer 3
```

Saving the TypeScript file mutates **Layer 1** only. The database record (Layer 3) is unchanged. The live site (Layer 4) is therefore unchanged. The route is an imperative script disguised as a file; it has no effect until it is invoked.

This is structurally identical to **ENG-204** from the engineering feedback log, where the `update-elan/route.ts` seed file still defined stale `imagePlaceholders` content вҖ” edits to the source were silently overwriting CMS deletions each time the seeder was called, and conversely, edits that were *never* called produced no effect at all. The root cause is the same: **a seeding route file is not a content record.**

The underlying engineering anti-pattern is captured in `docs/engineering.md` В§0 and the debugging spoke (`docs/engineering/debugging.md В§4.1`):

> **"Data sync issue вҖ” the UI reads from a different source than what was modified."** вҖ” `docs/engineering/debugging.md В§4.1, cause #1`

> **"Every data source must have exactly one canonical definition."** вҖ” `docs/engineering.md В§0`

The seeding route creates a second, non-canonical place where content *appears* to live, which is why this confusion recurs.

---

## Fix вҖ” Three options, in order of preference

### Option A: Use the Payload Admin UI directly (fastest, always correct)

This is the correct workflow for all content iteration. The admin panel is the canonical write path.

1. Navigate to `http://localhost:4000/admin` (or `https://new.yilangao.com/admin` in production).
2. Go to **Collections вҶ’ Projects вҶ’ ETRO Framework**.
3. Edit the **Intro Blurb body**, **Scope Statement**, and each **Section body** directly in the inline Lexical editor.
4. Click **Save**. The change is in the database immediately.
5. Reload the case study page вҖ” the new content is live.

No code changes, no route invocation, no deployment required.

### Option B: Invoke the seeding route (if you want the route to be the source of record)

If you specifically want the TypeScript file to be the authoritative content source (e.g. for reproducible seeding of a blank database), you must **call the route** after editing the file. The edit to `route.ts` is not enough on its own.

```bash
# Dev server must be running on port 4000
curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro
```

Then verify the database was actually updated:

```bash
# Check the record was written
curl -s "http://localhost:4000/api/projects?where[slug][equals]=etro-framework&depth=1" \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A2 '"introBlurbBody"\|"scopeStatement"'
```

Then reload the page and confirm the new text appears.

### Option C: Delete the seeding route (recommended long-term hygiene)

Seeding routes are bootstrapping tools. Once the CMS record exists and the inline-edit system is active, they have no ongoing value вҖ” and as ENG-204 showed, they become active liabilities (calling them overwrites intentional CMS edits). Once the ETRO record is stable:

```bash
# Remove the seeding route to eliminate the false dual-source
rm src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts
```

Content thereafter lives only in the database. There is no confusion about which "source" is authoritative.

---

## Correct Workflow for All Future Content Work

The mental model is: **the database is the content. The Payload admin (or inline-edit UI) is the pen. Source files are the rendering engine, not the document.**

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  CONTENT ITERATION WORKFLOW                                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  1. Open /admin (or use inline-edit on the live page)          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  2. Edit fields directly in the Payload UI                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  3. Click Save                                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  4. Reload the frontend page вҖ” content is live immediately     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  вң—  DO NOT edit .ts source files to change content            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  вң—  DO NOT edit seed/update routes and expect content to changeв”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  вң—  DO NOT check git history to find "where the content is"   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

Two specific content fields for the ETRO essay that map to Payload admin fields:

| What you want to change | Payload field name | Admin location |
|---|---|---|
| Intro blurb (the opening paragraph) | `introBlurbBody` | Projects вҶ’ ETRO вҶ’ Intro Blurb tab |
| Scope statement | `scopeStatement` | Projects вҶ’ ETRO вҶ’ Intro Blurb tab |
| Section body copy | `content[N].body` (Lexical block) | Projects вҶ’ ETRO вҶ’ Content tab, each section block |

---

## Principle

**Guardrail Engineering-12** already states this: *"Always trace data flow (source вҶ’ build вҶ’ server вҶ’ browser) when debugging visibility issues."* A content change that never reached the database will never reach the browser, regardless of how many times the source file is saved.

The seeding route is a write-once bootstrapper, not a content record. Treating it as a content record produces a phantom edit вҖ” work that appears done but has had zero effect on anything a user can see. Once a CMS project is seeded and operational, **the admin UI owns all content writes**.
